Bioregioning Tayside

type
organization
location_lat
56.5
background_section
## Background Tayside is a large swath of central Scotland organized around the River Tay catchment—from mountain headwaters to the estuary—where Atlantic salmon and varied ecosystems tie upland, strath, and coast into one hydrological story. The initiative treats that whole gradient as the practical frame for regeneration work. ### Origins and Network Bioregioning Tayside grew out of the Cateran Ecomuseum’s “museum in the landscape,” which prepared the lead organizers to think at bioregional scale. They stay connected across Tayside and with peer initiatives such as [[Bioregional Learning Centre South Devon]], which helped them get started. [The core team is small](https://erichtcatchment.scot/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Stocktake-and-Gap-Analysis_summary-compressed.pdf), held together by long collaboration, friendship, and shared care for the living world. A theory of change and catchment-wide strategic goals are still in draft. Learning is mostly organic—convened when an issue forces collective attention—and technical support is bought in when grants allow (for example, a leading Scottish food journalist serves as Food Ambassador).
Sources

temp
improper location but good for now?
description
Bioregioning Tayside takes a non-anthropocentric, place-based approach to regional regeneration, centering ecological systems alongside human communities. Guided by the principle "change the frame, change the story," the initiative aims to cultivate new narratives of place that support resilience in the face of climate disruption, biodiversity loss, and economic instability.
id
bioregioning-tayside
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Priorities on the horizon include deeper mapping (including carbon sequestration options), a possible **nature-finance aggregation** platform so varied restoration projects can connect to capital, regenerative tourism, and more intentional choices about digital tools—alongside persistent questions of skills and governance. [Funding remains the binding constraint](https://erichtcatchment.scot/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Stocktake-and-Gap-Analysis_summary-compressed.pdf): work is overwhelmingly grant-funded, nature-finance experiments are fragile under policy and corporate uncertainty, and no dedicated bioregional financial institutions exist yet. Engagement has been project-led and trust-based; a full bioregion-wide visioning process is still pending. Land and power in Scotland, thin investment in relationships, capacity gaps for restoration at pace, burnout, and the sheer complexity of restoration all slow change—yet visible projects and community voice are shifting what Tayside believes is possible, with arts and culture seen as under-used levers when resources allow.
website
https://bioregioningtayside.scot/
bioregional_context
A substantial region in Central Scotland centered around the River Tay catchment system, extending from mountains through to the Tay estuary. The area encompasses diverse landscapes, including one of Europe's most important Atlantic salmon spawning grounds. Their bioregion represents a significant portion of central Scotland's landmass, with varied ecosystems from highland to coastal areas.
